senses.title: book_n_1
senses.definition: a set of printed pages that are held together in a cover so that you can read them

examples.text: I’ve just started reading a book by Graham Greene.
a cookery book
a book about cats

senses.title: book_n_2
senses.definition: a set of sheets of paper held together in a cover so that you can write on them

examples.text: a black address book
a notebook

senses.title: book_n_3
senses.definition: a set of things such as stamps, matches, or tickets, held together inside a paper cover

examples.text: a cheque book

senses.title: book_n_4
senses.definition: a) written records of the financial accounts of a business b) the names of people who use a company’s services, or who are sent by a company to work for other people

examples.text: An accountant will examine the company’s books.
a small firm that is having problems balancing the books (=keeping its profits and spending equal)
They have £50 billion worth of orders on the books.
an agent with a lot of popular actors on his books

senses.title: book_n_5
senses.definition: exactly according to rules or instructions

examples.text: She feels she has to go by the book and can’t use her creativity.
The police were careful to do everything by the book.

senses.title: book_n_6
senses.definition: a subject that you do not understand or know anything about

examples.text: Chemistry is a closed book to me.

senses.title: book_n_7
senses.definition: used to say that someone is pleased or annoyed with you

senses.title: book_n_8
senses.definition: if a law is on the books, it is part of the set of laws in a country, town, area etc

senses.title: book_n_9
senses.definition: one of the parts that a very large book such as the Bible is divided into

examples.text: the Book of Isaiah

senses.title: book_n_10
senses.definition: said when giving your opinion

examples.text: In my book, nothing is more important than football.

senses.title: book_n_11
senses.definition: to punish someone for breaking laws or rules, especially when you have been trying to punish them for a long time

examples.text: War criminals must be brought to book.

senses.title: book_v_1
senses.definition: to make arrangements to stay in a place, eat in a restaurant, go to a theatre etc at a particular time in the future

examples.text: Have you booked a holiday this year?
The flight was already fully booked (=no more seats were available).
To get tickets, you have to book in advance.
The show’s booked solid (=all the tickets have been sold) until February.

senses.title: book_v_2
senses.definition: to arrange for someone such as a singer to perform on a particular date

examples.text: The band was booked for a benefit show in Los Angeles.

senses.title: book_v_3
senses.definition: a) if a hotel, restaurant etc is booked up, there are no more rooms, places, seats etc still available b) if someone is booked up, they are extremely busy and have arranged a lot of things they must do

examples.text: The courses quickly get booked up.
I’m all booked up this week – can we get together next Friday?

senses.title: book_v_4
senses.definition: to arrange for someone to go to a hotel, fly on a plane etc

examples.text: I’ve booked you a flight on Saturday.
I’ll book you in at the Hilton.

senses.title: book_v_5
senses.definition: to put someone’s name officially in police records, along with the charge made against them

examples.text: Smith was booked on suspicion of attempted murder.

senses.title: book_v_6
senses.definition: when a referee in a sports game books a player who has broken the rules, he or she officially writes down the player’s name in a book as a punishment

senses.title: separate_adj_1
senses.definition: different

examples.text: Use separate knives for raw and cooked meat.
My wife and I have separate bank accounts.

senses.title: separate_adj_2
senses.definition: not related to or not affected by something else

examples.text: That’s a separate issue.
He was attacked on two separate occasions.
He tries to keep his professional life completely separate from his private life.

senses.title: separate_adj_3
senses.definition: not joined to or touching something else

examples.text: The gym and the sauna are in separate buildings.
Keep the fish separate from the other food.

senses.title: separate_adj_4
senses.definition: a) if people go their separate ways, they stop being friends or lovers b) if people who have been travelling together go their separate ways, they start travelling in different directions

senses.title: separate_v_1
senses.definition: if something separates two places or two things, it is between them so that they are not touching each other

examples.text: The lighthouse is separated from the land by a wide channel.

senses.title: separate_v_2
senses.definition: to divide or split into different parts, or to make something do this

examples.text: This will keep your dressing from separating.
At this point, the satellite separates from its launcher.
Separate the students into four groups.
First, separate the eggs (=divide the white part from the yellow part).

senses.title: separate_v_3
senses.definition: if two people who are married or have been living together separate, they start to live apart

examples.text: Jill and John separated a year ago.
I decided to separate from my partner.

senses.title: separate_v_4
senses.definition: to recognize that one thing or idea is different from another

examples.text: She finds it difficult to separate fact from fantasy.

senses.title: separate_v_5
senses.definition: if people separate, or if someone or something separates them, they move apart

examples.text: Ed stepped in to separate the two dogs.
In the fog, they got separated from the group.

senses.title: separate_v_6
senses.definition: to be the quality or fact that makes someone or something different from other people or things

examples.text: The capacity to think separates humans from animals.

senses.title: separate_v_7
senses.definition: if an amount separates two things, one thing is better or older than the other by that amount

examples.text: Three points now separate the two teams.

senses.title: separate_v_8
senses.definition: to show clearly which people are brave, strong, or skilled, and which are not
senses.lex_unit: separate the men from the boys

senses.title: separate_v_9
senses.definition: to find out which people are intelligent, skilful, successful etc, and which are not
senses.lex_unit: separate the sheep from the goats

examples.text: This test should really separate the sheep from the goats.

entries.inflections: (past tense drove /drəʊv $ droʊv/, past participle driven /ˈdrɪvən/)

senses.title: drive_v_1
senses.definition: a) to make a car, truck, bus etc move along b) if a car, truck etc drives somewhere, it moves there c) if people drive somewhere, they travel somewhere in a car d) to take someone somewhere in a car, truck etc

examples.text: I am planning to drive to Morocco next year.
the man driving the car
Can you drive?
So when did you learn to drive?
Bye! Drive carefully!
He drives 12 miles to work.
He drives (=has) a BMW estate.
After the accident, the other car just drove off.
Shall we drive or take the bus?
They drove back to Woodside.
She drove Anna to London.
I’ll drive you home.
I drove myself to hospital.

senses.title: drive_v_2
senses.definition: to force a person or animal to go somewhere

examples.text: Torrential rain drove the players off the course.
With a few loud whistles, they drove the donkeys out of the enclosure.

senses.title: drive_v_3
senses.definition: to strongly influence someone to do something

examples.text: The detective wondered what had driven Christine to phone her.
The noises in my head have nearly driven me to suicide.
Phil, driven by jealousy, started spying on his wife.

senses.title: drive_v_4
senses.definition: to make someone or something get into a bad or extreme state, usually an emotional one

examples.text: This cough is driving me mad!
examples.usage: drive somebody crazy/nuts/mad/insane (=make someone feel very annoyed)

examples.text: The mosquitoes drive me to distraction.
The factory had been driven into bankruptcy.

senses.title: drive_v_5
senses.definition: to hit or push something into something else

examples.text: We watched Dad drive the posts into the ground.
She drove her heels into the sand.

senses.title: drive_v_6
senses.definition: to make a person or animal work hard

examples.text: Don’t drive yourself too hard.

senses.title: drive_v_7
senses.definition: a) to move a ball etc forward in a game of baseball, football, golf etc by hitting or kicking it hard and fast b) to run with the ball towards the goal in sports such as basketball and American football

examples.text: He drove the ball into the corner of the net.

senses.title: drive_v_8
senses.definition: to provide the power for a vehicle or machine

examples.text: a petrol-driven lawn mower

senses.title: drive_v_9
senses.definition: if rain, snow, wind etc drives somewhere, it moves very quickly in that direction

examples.text: The rain was driving down hard.

senses.title: drive_v_10
senses.definition: to destroy an argument, plan etc completely
senses.lex_unit: drive a coach and horses through something

examples.text: The new bill will drive a coach and horses through recent trade agreements.

senses.title: drive_v_11
senses.definition: to make a large hole in something using heavy equipment or machinery

examples.text: They drove a tunnel through the mountains.

senses.title: drive_v_12
senses.definition: to make something completely clear to someone

examples.text: He didn’t have to drive the point home. The videotape had done that.

senses.title: drive_v_13
senses.definition: to do something that makes people disagree or start to dislike each other

examples.text: I don’t want to drive a wedge between you and your father.

senses.title: drive_n_1
senses.definition: a journey in a car

examples.text: Let’s go for a drive along the coast.
Taylor took me for a drive through the town.
It’s a two-hour drive from Calais to Thiepval.

senses.title: drive_n_2
senses.definition: a strong natural need or desire

examples.text: The treatment will not affect your sex drive.

senses.title: drive_n_3
senses.definition: the hard area or road between your house and the street

examples.text: He parked his car in the drive.

senses.title: drive_n_4
senses.definition: an effort to achieve something, especially an effort by an organization for a particular purpose

examples.text: a recruitment drive for new members
an economy drive (=effort to reduce spending)
a nationwide drive to crack down on crime

senses.title: drive_n_5
senses.definition: determination and energy to succeed

examples.text: Brian has got tremendous drive.

senses.title: drive_n_6
senses.definition: the power from an engine that makes the wheels of a vehicle go round

senses.title: drive_n_7
senses.definition: a piece of equipment in a computer that is used to get information from a disk or to store information on it

senses.title: drive_n_8
senses.definition: an act of hitting a ball hard, especially in tennis, baseball, or golf

examples.text: He hit a long, high drive to right field.

senses.title: drive_n_9
senses.definition: several military attacks

examples.text: a drive deep into enemy territory

senses.title: drive_n_10
senses.definition: when animals such as cows or sheep are brought together and made to move in a particular direction

senses.title: drive_n_11
senses.definition: a) to make a car, truck, bus etc move along b) if a car, truck etc drives somewhere, it moves there c) if people drive somewhere, they travel somewhere in a car d) to take someone somewhere in a car, truck etc

examples.text: I am planning to drive to Morocco next year.
the man driving the car
Can you drive?
So when did you learn to drive?
Bye! Drive carefully!
He drives 12 miles to work.
He drives (=has) a BMW estate.
After the accident, the other car just drove off.
Shall we drive or take the bus?
They drove back to Woodside.
She drove Anna to London.
I’ll drive you home.
I drove myself to hospital.

senses.title: Drive_
senses.definition: used in the names of roads

examples.text: 141 Park Drive
